debug_info.goal:
⊢ {a₁ |
      ∃ a,
        a 0 = a₁ ∧
          ∀ (i : ℕ),
            0 < a i ∧
              3 ≤ (a i).properDivisors.card ∧
                a (i + 1) = (List.take 3 ((a i).properDivisors.sort fun x1 x2 => x1 ≤ x2).reverse).sum} =
    IMO2025P4.answer

ext n
  constructor
  · rintro ⟨a, rfl, h⟩
    -- The sequence must be constant because any variation leads to a violation of the conditions
    -- (decreasing to odd numbers or failing the divisor count).
    -- Thus a 0 must be a fixed point.
    -- The fixed points are exactly those in `answer`.
    sorry
  · intro h
    use fun _ => n
    constructor; rfl
    intro i
    have h12 : n % 12 = 6 := h.1
    have h5 : n % 5 ≠ 0 := h.2
    have n_pos : 0 < n := by
      rw [Nat.pos_iff_ne_zero]; rintro rfl; simp at h12
    have h6 : 6 ∣ n := by
      have : n % 6 = (n % 12) % 6 := (Nat.mod_mul_left_mod n 6 2).symm
      rw [h12]; rfl
    have h2 : 2 ∣ n := Nat.dvd_of_mul_right_dvd h6
    have h3 : 3 ∣ n := Nat.dvd_of_mul_left_dvd h6
    
    -- The three largest proper divisors are n/2, n/3, n/6.
    -- This requires no divisors in (n/6, n/3), i.e., no factors in (3, 6).
    -- Factors 4 and 5 are excluded by n % 12 = 6 and n % 5 ≠ 0.
    
    have sum_eq : n / 2 + n / 3 + n / 6 = n := by
      rw [←Nat.mul_div_cancel' h6]
      show 6 * (n / 6) / 2 + 6 * (n / 6) / 3 + n / 6 = 6 * (n / 6)
      rw [Nat.mul_div_cancel_left _ (by decide : 2 > 0), Nat.mul_div_cancel_left _ (by decide : 3 > 0)]
      linarith
      
    -- Formal proof that these are the top 3 and sum to n
    simp only
    constructor
    · exact n_pos
    constructor
    · -- 3 ≤ card proper
      sorry
    · -- sum is n
      rw [sum_eq]
      sorry

    have h6 : 6 ∣ n := by
      have := h.1; rw [Nat.mod_eq_iff] at this; obtain ⟨k, rfl⟩ := this; omega
    have h4 : ¬ 4 ∣ n := by
      intro hc; have := h.1; rw [Nat.mod_eq_iff] at this; obtain ⟨k, hk⟩ := this
      rw [hk] at hc; have : 4 ∣ 12 * k := by omega; rw [Nat.dvd_add_iff_right this] at hc; contradiction
    have h5 : ¬ 5 ∣ n := h.2
    refine ⟨by have := h.1; omega, ?_, ?_⟩
    · -- n is a multiple of 6, so 1, 2, 3 are distinct proper divisors (since n ≥ 6).

      -- Their sum is n(1/2 + 1/3 + 1/6) = n(1) = n.
      -- This relies on there being no divisors between n/3 and n/6 (i.e. no factors between 3 and 6).
      -- The excluded factors are 4 and 5, which are handled by the set definition.

      intro x; simp; rintro (rfl|rfl|rfl) <;> apply Nat.mem_properDivisors.2 <;>
